question: Explain the primary purpose of the `static` keyword when applied to a variable in a Java class. Describe the key memory and access implications.
expectedPoints: A static variable is a class-level variable, not an instance-level variable.
Only a single copy of the variable is created and shared among all instances of the class.
It is stored in the method area (or a dedicated static memory section) of the JVM, not on the heap with object instances.
It can be accessed directly using the class name (e.g., `ClassName.variableName`) without needing to create an instance of the class.

question: Compare and contrast a static method with an instance method. Identify two key restrictions placed on static methods.
expectedPoints: An instance method operates on a specific object's state and requires an instance to be called.
A static method belongs to the class itself and can be called without any object instances.
A static method cannot use the `this` keyword, as there is no current instance for it to refer to.
A static method cannot directly access instance variables or call non-static methods; it can only directly access other static members.

question: What is a static block and when is it executed? Provide a simple use case for one.
expectedPoints: A static block is a block of code declared with the `static` keyword, used for initializing static variables.
It is executed exactly once when the class is loaded into memory by the JVM, before the creation of any instances or the execution of any static methods.
A common use case is to perform complex initialization for static variables that cannot be done in a single line, such as populating a static collection.
